target: Debug analysis findings and recommended fixes from earlier in this conversation

rootCauseClusters.name: Shallow Static Scan
rootCauseClusters.description: The debug analysis performed line-level static checks without architectural reasoning, missing data corruption (race condition), performance (sequential parsing), and unbounded growth (offset keys) issues that require understanding runtime behavior.

rootCauseClusters.name: Prior Work Amnesia
rootCauseClusters.description: The analysis did not check the existing challenge report (2026-03-07-challenge.json) before starting. This caused it to present a known finding as new (#3=console.warn), miss 4 unfixed HIGHs, and fail to identify the hourly bug as a regression from the previous fix commit.

rootCauseClusters.name: Severity Inversion
rootCauseClusters.description: Style nits (error: any, CRLF) received equal visual weight with a compile error, diluting the signal-to-noise ratio.

findings.description: Analysis missed the handleFileChange race condition (previous challenge HIGH #5, still unfixed). Two concurrent async calls read the same shared currentBuckets, await parsing (yielding to event loop), then both write back. Second write silently overwrites first merge, losing usage data.
findings.suggestion: Add a serial processing queue so handleFileChange calls execute sequentially. Chain promises or use a mutex.

findings.description: Analysis missed sequential file parsing (previous challenge HIGH #9, still unfixed). parseAllSessions processes files sequentially with no parallelism. Users with 100+ files experience 10-30s UI freeze on activation, blocking all extensions on the shared host.
findings.suggestion: Batch files into parallel groups (10 concurrent). Add CancellationToken support. Report progress to status bar.

findings.description: Analysis missed offset tracker key pollution (previous challenge HIGH #8, still unfixed). globalState keys accumulate forever with no pruning. After months of use, thousands of stale keys degrade VS Code startup performance.
findings.suggestion: On activation, prune keys whose files no longer exist. Add max-keys limit or TTL.

findings.description: Finding #1 (missing hourly in resetState) was correctly identified as a bug but not identified as a REGRESSION from commit 6fe75e1. The previous challenge fix added hourly to the constructor (line 46) but missed resetState(). The analysis should have checked git blame and audited all fix sites.
findings.suggestion: When reviewing recently-fixed code, always check git diff to identify what changed and verify all change sites are consistent.

findings.description: Finding #3 (console.warn in paths.ts) was already documented as MEDIUM #16 in the previous challenge. The analysis presented it without referencing prior work, causing duplicate investigation.
findings.suggestion: Read existing review artifacts before starting any new analysis.

findings.description: Finding #2 was labeled 'unhandled promise rejection' but fetchApiUsage uses new Promise((resolve) => ...) and only calls resolve(), never reject(). The promise structurally cannot reject. The .then() callback could theoretically throw, but buildStatusBarData is a pure computation and statusBar.update sets simple properties. This is MEDIUM at most, not the severity implied.
findings.suggestion: When flagging promise issues, read the implementation to verify whether rejection is actually possible.

findings.description: Finding #4 (catch error: any) and Finding #5 (CRLF line endings) are trivial style issues that received equal visual weight with the compile error, diluting the signal. Biome lint passed clean. The any type is used specifically for ENOENT code access.
findings.suggestion: Separate real bugs from style preferences. Present them in distinct severity tiers.

findings.description: Analysis praised 'mergeTimeBuckets deep-copies to avoid mutation bugs' and 'dispose() properly clears debounce timers' without noting these were RECENT fixes from the previous challenge. This creates false confidence that these are original good engineering rather than patches over recently-discovered problems.
findings.suggestion: When praising code patterns, check git blame. Note if patterns were recently added as bug fixes.

planAttacks.attack: The debug analysis used explorer agents that generated findings at explorer speed without verifying against prior work, producing 2 findings that were already known and 1 false positive out of 5 total.
planAttacks.defense: Finding #1 (missing hourly) is a genuine compile error that blocks build and would crash on user action. This single finding justifies the analysis cost.

planAttacks.attack: The 'Good patterns observed' section praised code that was recently bug-fixed, creating false confidence about codebase health.
planAttacks.defense: The patterns ARE good now, regardless of their history. But the missing context about 4 unfixed HIGHs makes the overall health assessment misleading.

planAttacks.attack: Offering to fix all 5 findings would fix 1 real bug, add dead code (.catch on non-rejecting promise), potentially break initialization order (Logger in paths.ts), and waste time on 2 non-issues.
planAttacks.defense: The user wasn't told which fixes to accept. The offer was to fix 'confirmed issues' - the user would have approved each fix individually.
